skill.name: adaptyv
skill.description: Guides protein design teams through Adaptyv cloud lab workflows for experiment submission, result tracking, assay selection, and sequence optimization.
skill.summary: Plan, submit, and monitor Adaptyv protein validation experiments from AI assistants.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, credential, network, filesystem, and weak cryptography findings. Review found the command execution and weak cryptography hits are false positives from Markdown fences, inline code, and scientific terminology, while the network, environment variable, webhook, and local file write examples are legitimate for this Adaptyv API skill. No prompt injection text or confirmed malicious intent was found, but users should treat the skill as medium risk because it sends protein sequences and bearer-token authenticated requests to external services.

medium_findings.title: External API Requests With Bearer Tokens
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to load ADAPTYV_API_KEY, place it in an Authorization bearer header, and submit requests to the Adaptyv API. This is expected for the skill, but it exposes secrets and experiment data to an external cloud service if users run the examples.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code examples clearly construct bearer-token requests to external HTTPS endpoints. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so this is a real but legitimate data exposure risk rather than confirmed malicious activity.

medium_findings.title: Protein Sequences Are Sent To External Services
medium_findings.description: Examples submit protein sequences and metadata to Adaptyv and also show NetSolP solubility prediction through a third-party service. Users working with proprietary sequences must review data sharing and consent requirements before using these workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced examples directly post sequence data to external endpoints. This is consistent with cloud lab and prediction workflows, but the data sensitivity risk is clear.

medium_findings.title: Webhook And Download URL Handling Requires Care
medium_findings.description: The API reference and examples include webhook callbacks and result download URLs. The documentation recommends HTTPS and signature verification, but user implementations must enforce those controls to avoid spoofed notifications or unsafe result handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Webhook guidance is explicit and includes security notes, which lowers concern. Risk remains because users may copy examples without implementing verification correctly.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby backtick execution findings, but reviewed locations are Markdown code fences or inline code examples. No Ruby execution context or automated command runner was found in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation delimiters around examples, not executable Ruby code. This makes the command-execution finding a high-confidence false positive.

low_findings.title: Local Result Writes Are User-Directed Examples
low_findings.description: The examples create output directories and write experiment results or experiment metadata to local JSON files. This is normal result caching behavior, but users should choose trusted output paths and avoid writing sensitive data into shared folders.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are visible and limited to result persistence examples. There is no evidence of hidden file access or destructive filesystem behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography And Reconnaissance Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak cryptography and reconnaissance findings map to biology terms, assay documentation, generic URLs, or response examples. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation, system probing, or network scanning logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations contain scientific prose and placeholder result links, not crypto APIs or reconnaissance commands. The analyzer appears to have matched substrings outside security-relevant context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Authentication In Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples read an API key from the environment and attach it to outbound Authorization headers. This pattern is appropriate for authenticated APIs but must be protected from logging, screenshots, and accidental commits.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token-handling pattern is explicit and confirmed by code examples. It is not malicious, but it is a sensitive operational pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Submission Of Biological Designs
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples submit sequence payloads to Adaptyv and NetSolP endpoints. Users should classify sequence data before running these examples because biological designs may be confidential.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The outbound sequence submission is directly shown in the documentation. The risk depends on user data sensitivity and service authorization.

content.value_statement: Protein teams need a reliable bridge from computational design to wet-lab validation. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan Adaptyv experiments, use the API, track status, and interpret expected result formats.
actual_capabilities: Explains Adaptyv authentication with ADAPTYV_API_KEY and bearer headers.
Provides examples for submitting single and batch protein experiments.
Describes binding, expression, thermostability, and enzyme activity assays.
Shows status polling, webhooks, result downloads, and retry patterns.
Guides sequence optimization with NetSolP, SoluProt, SolubleMPNN, ESM, ipTM, and pSAE.
Documents expected API endpoints, request fields, response fields, errors, and rate limits.
limitations: The platform is described as alpha or beta, so API behavior may change.
The skill provides documentation and examples, not direct lab access by itself.
Users need their own Adaptyv API credentials and service approval.
Computational sequence predictions are probabilistic and require experimental validation.

use_cases.title: Submit a Variant Library
use_cases.description: Prepare FASTA sequences, choose an assay type, add metadata, and submit variants for wet-lab validation.

use_cases.title: Automate Experiment Tracking
use_cases.description: Build scripts that check experiment status, receive webhook notifications, and download completed result files.

use_cases.title: Prioritize Designs Before Testing
use_cases.description: Use solubility, stability, and sequence-likelihood workflows to select better candidates before submitting experiments.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an Assay
prompt_templates.prompt: I have protein variants for [goal]. Help me choose the most suitable Adaptyv experiment type and list the required inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Submission
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Adaptyv submission plan for these FASTA sequences: [sequences]. Include experiment type, metadata, and validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Track Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a workflow to monitor Adaptyv experiment [experiment_id], handle rate limits, and save completed results safely.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Before Lab Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this protein design campaign and propose an optimization pipeline using solubility, ESM, structure, and Adaptyv validation steps.

output_examples.input: Select an experiment type for antibody variants targeting PD-L1.
output_examples.output: Recommended output would compare binding and expression assays, identify required target information, and suggest controls for the submission.

output_examples.input: Plan status tracking for a long-running Adaptyv experiment.
output_examples.output: Recommended output would describe webhook setup, fallback polling intervals, retry behavior, result download timing, and local storage considerations.

output_examples.input: Prioritize 200 designed enzyme variants before submission.
output_examples.output: Recommended output would propose solubility filtering, sequence-likelihood scoring, control selection, batch sizing, and metadata fields for traceability.

best_practices: Store ADAPTYV_API_KEY outside source control and avoid printing bearer tokens.
Validate FASTA formatting, controls, metadata, and assay type before submission.
Use HTTPS webhooks with signature verification and keep result files in protected storage.
anti_patterns: Do not submit confidential protein sequences without approval for external processing.
Do not hardcode API keys, webhook secrets, or experiment credentials in scripts.
Do not rely only on computational optimization when experimental validation is required.

faq.question: Does this skill run experiments directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for using Adaptyv workflows and APIs. Users need their own approved account and credentials.

faq.question: Which assays are covered?
faq.answer: The documentation covers binding assays, expression testing, thermostability measurements, and enzyme activity assays.

faq.question: Can it help with protein sequence optimization?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes workflows using solubility prediction, SolubleMPNN, ESM scoring, interface metrics, and experimental validation.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: The examples expect an Adaptyv API key stored as ADAPTYV_API_KEY and sent as a bearer token.

faq.question: Is it safe for proprietary sequences?
faq.answer: Use caution. The workflows send sequences and metadata to external services, so users should confirm data sharing rules first.

faq.question: How long do results take?
faq.answer: The skill states that Adaptyv results are typically delivered in about 21 days after submission.
